1.description: Selling propensity based on return and dividend yield. This graph shows a contour plot of the residual selling propensity after controlling for investor-specific holding period, past volatility, number of positions, and account fixed effects. Thex-axis shows the total return on a position, whereas they-axis shows the dividend yield. The color indicates the selling propensity shown in the legend to the right of the graph. The sample includes investors that display a disposition effect on prior trades. The range examined is returns from −5% to 5% and dividend-paying stocks with dividend yields less than or equal to 5%. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Changes in holdings for dividend-paying and nondividend-paying stocks. This graph shows the distribution of the change in the number of shares of a given holding from one report date to the next, for holdings that paid a dividend between the two report days (left graph) and those that did not (right graph). Panel A examines holdings changes for mutual funds, and Panel B examines holdings changes for institutions. The maroon and green bars represent the number of holdings with the exact same number of positions from quarter to quarter. The blue bars represent changes in the number of position in 100s. Bars are centered atx. To the right of the maroon bar are changes from(x−50,x+50]and to the left[x−50,x+50). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Difference between actual change in mutual fund and institutional holdings and amount corresponding to full reinvestment of dividends. This graph examines the amount by which changes in mutual fund and institutional holdings differ from the amount corresponding to dividend reinvestment, given that the investor made some change in holdings. For holdings that paid a dividend during the quarter, we compute the actual change in holdings minus the change in holdings that would occur if the dividend were immediately reinvested into the stock on the payment date. As a result, a fund that exactly reinvests the dividend will show a difference of zero. The figure plots the distribution of this difference for all dividend-paying fund holdings where the amount to be reinvested was at least 100 shares. The maroon bar represents a difference between −99 and +100 (i.e., the fund invested within 100 shares of the amount of the dividend). The blue bars are difference amounts binned into units of 100 shares. We exclude observations for which the fund made no change in shares. Only funds with a difference of report days between 60 and 100 calendar days are included. Panel A examines the holdings of mutual funds while Panel B examines the holdings of institutional investors. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Dividend induced drift. This graph shows the change over time in portfolio weights of dividend-paying positions relative to nondividend-paying positions. The dependent variable is the portfolio weight at timetdivided by the portfolio weight when the position was opened att=0. This is regressed on dummy variables for time since opened and the interaction between this variable and a dummy variable equal to 1 if the position paid a dividend in the holding period. The graph is of the interaction coefficient, which is the difference in the change in portfolio weight for dividend-paying positions compared to nondividend-paying positions. For individual investors, the time period is monthly, whereas for mutual funds and institutions, it is quarterly. The change in portfolio weight variable is winsorized at the 99th percentile and positions are excluded if the initial or current portfolio weight is 1 or if the initial portfolio weight is below 0.0001 of the portfolio. Standard errors are clustered by time period and investor, fund, or institution, and the 95% confidence intervals are graphed. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Dividend interim returns over time. This graph shows a local linear plot of the interim period return (the cumulative characteristic-adjusted return after a dividend announcement and before the ex-date) over time. The green-shaded area coincides roughly with the tech boom from January 1995 through April 2000. The blue-shaded area represents low interest rates from January 2009 through June 2016, when the federal funds rate was below 0.50. The gray area indicates the 90% confidence interval. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
